4. Geometric concepts: what determines location quality?

children.label: 4. Geometric concepts: what determines location quality?

children.value: more on geometry than on the number of stations

children.html: Δϕ\Delta\phiΔϕ

children.value: is the largest angle (in plan view) between adjacent stations as seen from the epicenter.
Most seismic networks require gap < 180° for “good” locations.
is the horizontal distance from epicenter to the closest station.
, rays exit at shallow takeoff angles → poor depth resolution
, rays exit steeply → good depth leverage

from the source to the seismic array.
(rays exit in many directions): Good 3D constraint
(all rays nearly horizontal): Depth/origin time trade-off

Station (surface)
           ↑ steep ray
          /

        /
       ★ Source (depth)
      /

    /   poor depth sensitivity

Station (far)
✗ Poor depth (narrow aperture, origin time coupling)
Mix of nearby and distant stations for balanced 3D resolution.
5. Why depth is the hardest parameter

children.label: 5. Why depth is the hardest parameter

children.value: Travel times are insensitive to depth changes unless:
For an array of distant stations, the following

children.html: vvv

children.value: increases with depth) → shorter travel time
→ all arrivals shift earlier by same amount
, creating an elongated error ellipse in the depth–

children.value: If you use a model that is too fast, you’ll locate events
If too slow, you’ll locate events
Fix depth at typical crustal values (e.g., 10 km) if geometry is poor, to avoid trading off depth uncertainty into horizontal position errors.

children.value: Without relocating, will your computed origin time be too early or too late?

Two earthquakes occur at the same location, separated by 1 hour. You have excellent azimuthal coverage and 12 stations.
If all stations show the second event arriving 0.5 seconds
than the first, what can you conclude?
What if all stations show the second event arriving later,
one station shows it arriving earlier?
You are locating a cluster of aftershocks from a mainshock. With standard absolute location:
Your locations have ERH = 2 km and ERZ = 5 km
Using double-difference relative location, what order-of-magnitude improvement would you expect?
What common errors are canceled by relative location methods?
9. Extensions and what we deliberately did not do

children.label: 9. Extensions and what we deliberately did not do

children.value: Formal inverse problem framework (χ², residuals, statistical measures)
What we deliberately did not do

children.label: What we deliberately did not do

children.value: (using common source clusters to refine structure)
but would obscure the core geometric lessons.
When using published earthquake catalogs (USGS, ISC, regional networks), check:
: Should be comparable to or less than depth for reliable depth
: < 0.5 s for local, < 1.0 s for regional
: “f” or “*” means depth was not free parameter
When designing a permanent seismic network:
: 6 well-placed stations beat 20 poorly distributed ones
for depth resolution (DMIN < typical focal depth)
: Do you have stations within the rupture zone?
In the companion notebook, we will:
Implement grid search to visualize the nonlinear objective function
Systematically explore geometric failure modes (gap, depth, etc.)
: Takeoff angles and ray parameters directly determine location resolution
: Location is the dual problem; identical inverse theory framework
: Accurate locations are prerequisite for moment tensor inversion
: Formal inverse problems, residuals, χ², L1 vs L2 norms
Lee, W. H. K., & Stewart, S. W. (1981).
Principles and Applications of Microearthquake Networks

Husen, S., & Hardebeck, J. L. (2010). Earthquake location accuracy.
Community Online Resource for Statistical Seismicity Analysis
Waldhauser, F., & Ellsworth, W. L. (2000). A double-difference earthquake location algorithm: Method and application to the northern Hayward fault, California.
